ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for Kadai Mushroom Masala. Closest defensible match is 'mushroom dish' (FOODON_03311069). Exact term absent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Kadai Mushroom Masala curry preparation. A0CKP (Mushrooms, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. Spiced curry preparation not separately coded.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Kadai Mushroom Masala or equivalent Indian mushroom curry preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Kadai Mushroom Masala is approximately 250 g, served as a main course alongside naan, roti, or steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Mushroom curry, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition) and USDA FoodData Central: Mushrooms, white, cooked, boiled (FDC ID 169251) used for mushroom base; spiced tomato-onion gravy component estimated from ICMR-NIN composite curry data
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Kadai Mushroom Masala specifically. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Mushroom curry' composite entry (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2017) which covers button mushrooms cooked in a spiced tomato-onion gravy with oil. This is the closest approved primary source match. USDA FDC mushroom cooked data used to cross-validate mushroom base macros. Serving-level values scaled to 250 g portion.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable curry and mushroom-based dishes — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for vegetable curry (GI ~35) and cooked mushrooms (GI ~10–15); weighted composite applied
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Kadai Mushroom Masala in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 based on: (1) cooked mushrooms have a very low GI (~10–15, University of Sydney GI Database); (2) spiced tomato-onion gravy with minimal starch contributes a low-moderate GI (~40–45); (3) the dish is predominantly non-starchy vegetables with moderate oil content. Composite weighted GI of 35 is a defensible low-GI estimate. GL per serving = (35 × 6.0 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2.5 serving factor) / 100 = 5.25, rounded to 5.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~775 mg per 250 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g restaurant serving provides approximately 775 mg sodium, representing about 34% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended by clinical guidelines; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load per serving (~5.3)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Kadai Mushroom Masala has a low GI of approximately 35 and a glycemic load of ~5.3 per 250 g serving, making it a suitable choice for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or plain naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (~0.6 g per 100 g), zero cholesterol, and high proportion of unsaturated fats from cooking oil
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat and contains no cholesterol, with the predominant fat being unsaturated; however, total fat from restaurant oil usage (~4.2 g per 100 g) should be monitored in individuals following a calorie-restricted cardiac diet, and sodium intake should be managed as noted above.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kadai Mushroom Masala is a popular North Indian vegetarian curry prepared by sautéing button mushrooms, sliced onions, and bell peppers (capsicum) in a kadai (a traditional Indian wok) with a spiced, tangy tomato-based sauce. The dish is seasoned with whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, garam masala, red chilli powder, and kasuri methi (dried fenugreek leaves), which give it a robust, aromatic flavour profile with moderate heat and a slightly smoky character. The mushrooms provide a meaty, umami-rich texture that absorbs the masala sauce well, while the capsicum adds crunch and sweetness. Kadai Mushroom Masala is fully vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free in its standard preparation, making it suitable for a wide range of dietary requirements. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-GI dish (~72 kcal per 100 g, GI ~35) with moderate protein (~3.1 g per 100 g) from mushrooms, low saturated fat, and useful amounts of B vitamins, potassium, and vitamin C. The sodium content from restaurant preparation (~310 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. It is widely served in North Indian restaurants across Singapore and is typically accompanied by naan, roti, or steamed basmati rice. The dish is also known as Kadhai Mushroom, Karahi Mushroom, or Mushroom Kadai.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Kadai Mushroom?
Is Kadai Mushroom good for diabetics?
What is the difference between Kadai Mushroom and Mushroom Masala?

Can I eat Kadai Mushroom if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or USDA entry exists specifically for Kadai Mushroom Masala. Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN mushroom curry composite entry (Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, 2017), which is the closest approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database vegetable curry and mushroom data. Confidence is medium rather than high because exact-dish data is unavailable in any approved primary source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN 'Mushroom curry' composite data (2017 edition) and cross-validated against USDA FDC cooked mushroom data. No exact Kadai Mushroom Masala entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 3. Sodium value (~310 mg per 100 g) reflects typical restaurant preparation; home-cooked versions may have significantly lower sodium. 4. The dish is vegan and dairy-free in standard preparation; some restaurant versions may add cream or butter, which would alter fat, saturated fat, and dairy-free status. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients in standard preparation; mushrooms do not provide meaningful B12. 6. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as the dish contains no animal-derived ingredients in standard preparation. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero for standard vegetable oil preparation without hydrogenated fats. 8. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311069 and FoodEx2 A0CKP are closest_parent matches only; no exact ontology code exists for this dish.
